We often hear from teams looking for a smoother path from open-source theory to real-world clinical practice. If that resonates with you, I highly recommend exploring the Pharmaverse Examples site. 

Think of it as a hands-on guide for your team that goes beyond standard documentation, showing you exactly how to orchestrate packages like {admiral}, {sdtm.oak}, and {gtsummary} into cohesive, reproducible pipelines. 

It’s a great way to walk through your project from raw EDC intake to final, submission-ready outputs with a bit more clarity and confidence

P.S. While we usually share links directly from the Posit blog, we are experimenting with a new format to make it easier for our community to discover and access the deep-dive content we create. Enjoy! ☺️

#pharmaverse #clinicalreporting #rstats #biostatistics #datascience

In the high-stakes world of pharmaceutical clinical reporting, moving to open source often feels like a leap into the unknown. We are excited to share the Pharmaverse Examples Site. This library is a comprehensive, interactive resource designed to bridge that gap for biostatisticians and clinical programmers.

✨Pharmaverse Examples Site: https://lnkd.in/gRXksuaa

Transitioning from legacy systems to R or Python requires more than just new code; it requires a validated, integrated workflow. 

While individual packages like {admiral} or {sdtm.oak} offer robust documentation, the Examples Site serves as a "living book" that demonstrates how these tools work together, working from raw EDC data to submission-ready outputs.

Here are some more of the awesome things the Pharmaverse Examples Site simplifies in the exploration of open-source clinical reporting:

🛝 A Pre-Loaded Playground: Through integration with Posit Cloud, users can launch a fully configured RStudio environment in seconds. This eliminates the barrier of local software installation, allowing you to run end-to-end clinical scripts immediately.

🔄 Structured Lifecycle Workflows: The site mirrors the standard clinical programming journey. You can explore SDTM mapping via {sdtm.oak}, ADaM derivations using the {admiral} engine, and TLG generation for both static and interactive reporting.

📖 Regulatory Traceability: Beyond code, the resource highlights compliance-ready practices, such as generating execution logs with {logrx} and formatting outputs like XPT files for FDA and EMA submissions.

🩻 Realistic Clinical Data: Users can experiment with synthetic, industry-standard datasets, such as {pharmaversesdtm} and {pharmaverseadam}, to see exactly how treatment start dates or population flags are derived in a reproducible setting.

The Pharmaverse is a collaborative, PHUSE-supported community effort. We invite you to explore these examples, contribute your own workflows via GitHub, and join the conversation on the Pharmaverse Slack!

💻 Pharmaverse Slack: https://lnkd.in/gtJvVGb2

➡️Read our recent blog on Pharmaverse Examples Site written by Ross Farrugia at Roche: https://lnkd.in/gJw6mfm8

Announcing mirai 2.6.0: Enhanced Parallel and Asynchronous Computing for R

We are pleased to announce the release of mirai 2.6.0, a significant update to R’s framework for parallel and asynchronous computing. This release addresses the common challenge of moving from a local prototype to enterprise-level deployment without rewriting code 💪 

• New HTTP Launcher: A flagship feature that allows you to deploy daemons to cloud and enterprise platforms via HTTP API calls. This includes direct integration with Posit Workbench, where daemons can now be launched as managed jobs with a single function call.
• C-Level Dispatcher: To minimize task overhead, the dispatcher has been re-implemented in C within nanonext. This reduces per-task dispatch latency to the tens of microseconds.
• Efficient Pattern Handling: The introduction of race_mirai() enables "process-as-completed" patterns. This allows you to handle results the moment they are ready rather than waiting for the slowest task in a batch to finish.
• Improved Debugging and Sync: A new synchronous mode facilitates interactive debugging using standard tools like browser(), while the .min argument in everywhere() ensures remote daemons are fully synchronized and ready before work begins.

Learn more about mirai 2.6.0 and its capabilities: https://lnkd.in/ggC559Sv

dplyr 1.2.0 is out now! This large release of dplyr comes with two sets of exciting features:

✔️ filter_out(), the missing complement to filter(), and accompanying when_any() and when_all() helpers.

✔️ recode_values(), replace_values(), and replace_when(), three new functions that join case_when() to create a cohesive family of powerful tools for recoding and replacing.

Cast your vote below and comment your favorite features!

"I feel like working with agents is a lot more productive, but a lot less fun." 
Charlie Marsh tweeted this and people won't stop quoting it back at him. On The Test Set, he gets into why — and it's not really about fun. It's about the vibe being off. We're in this weird in-between where engineers are stitching together agent outputs, copy-pasting PR comments into Claude Code, and reviewing code they didn't write but are somehow responsible for. It's giving duct tape. Charlie says he's hopeful we'll figure out what building software actually looks like in this new era — but right now? We're not there. He also drops that his opinions on literally all of this are radically different from four months ago, which honestly says everything about how fast this is moving. Full conversation now live.

We are pleased to share the latest edition of posit::glimpse(), our roundup of significant news and product releases for the Posit open-source community 🎉 

Key highlights from this edition include:

• ggplot2 v4.0.0: A major milestone for R’s premier visualization package. This release modernizes the internal engine and introduces "role-oriented styling," allowing you to define foreground and background colors more intuitively
• dplyr v1.2.0: Significant performance optimizations make functions like if_else() and case_when() up to 30x faster. New verbs like filter_out() and logical helpers when_any() / when_all() streamline complex data filtering tasks.
• ellmer v0.4.0: Enhanced support for working with LLMs (Claude and OpenAI), featuring new capabilities for file uploads, web search tools, and automated data frame schema descriptions.
• Positron 2026.02.0: Our next-generation IDE now includes a native editor for Jupyter Notebooks.

Additionally, registration is now officially open for posit::conf(2026), taking place in Houston from September 14–16!

Learn more about these updates and the future of our tools: https://lnkd.in/ga_SsfJJ

Announcing plumber2 0.2.0: Enhanced Observability and Authentication for R APIs

We are pleased to announce the release of plumber2 0.2.0, a significant update to the modern web server framework for R users.

Following the initial release of plumber2 as the successor to the original plumber package, this version introduces critical features designed to bring observability and robust authentication to R-based APIs. 

This update addresses the growing need for better monitoring and access control in deployed environments so that R APIs can be managed with the same rigor as any other microservice in a modern stack.

• OpenTelemetry (OTEL) Support: Native integration with OpenTelemetry to capture traces, logs, and metrics. Your R APIs can now integrate with observability backends like Jaeger, Prometheus, and Grafana Cloud.
• Integrated Authentication: The new version adds dedicated support for authentication and authorization. It includes "guards" for common schemes such as Basic, Bearer (token-based), and OAuth 2.0/OpenID Connect.
• Performance Optimization: Beyond new features, this update includes tangible performance improvements to the underlying request handling.

Learn more about plumber2 0.2.0 and its capabilities: https://lnkd.in/gvDVRDBS

Big news for Jupyter users 👋

We just announced the alpha release of the Positron Notebook Editor, built to give .ipynb files the first-class experience they deserve.

Jupyter notebooks sit at the center of modern data science. With this release, we’re taking a big step forward in Positron’s support of the Jupyter ecosystem, combining the exploratory simplicity people love about notebooks with the capabilities of a professional data science IDE.

We rebuilt the notebook experience in Positron to be batteries-included, so things work out of the box without having to manually configure extensions just to get started:

- Explore, sort, and filter tabular data with the Data Explorer
- Inspect active variables instantly without print statements
- Work across Python and R environments
- Access IDE capabilities built specifically for data science
- Use an assistant that understands your notebook context

This is an early alpha, and we’re actively looking for community feedback to help shape what comes next.

Announcing yaml12: High-Performance YAML 1.2 Parsing for R and Python

We are pleased to announce the release of yaml12 (for R) and py-yaml12 (for Python), two new packages designed to provide predictable, high-performance YAML 1.2 parsing and emitting.

These packages are specifically designed for those who use YAML for configuration files, front matter, or data interchange. While the existing yaml package in R remains maintained for those who rely on it, yaml12 offers a modernized alternative built on a shared Rust-based core.

• Built with Rust, the R package is approximately 2x faster than the original yaml package, while the Python version (py-yaml12) has demonstrated speeds over 50x faster than default PyYAML in common benchmarks.
• By following the YAML 1.2 specification, the packages provide stricter implicit conversions.
• Both the R and Python packages share a consistent API and design philosophy, making it easier for teams working in multi-language environments to maintain the same YAML behavior.
The library is designed to preserve mapping order and handle unhandled tags gracefully, ensuring that data written back to YAML retains its original structure and intent.

Learn more about the release and its technical capabilities on the Tidyverse blog: https://lnkd.in/gXMBxzdU

Announcing Fifty Releases of pointblank: A Major Milestone in Data Validation in Python!

We are pleased to announce the 50th release of pointblank for Python, a powerful framework designed to streamline data validation, profiling, and quality monitoring.

Data integrity is the cornerstone of reliable analysis. Whether you are using Polars and Pandas, you can maintain consistent validation standards across your entire organization.

• Define rigorous checks from simple range constraints to complex schema consistency using a fluent, chainable API.
• Automatically generate high-fidelity, interactive "data quality report cards" that turn technical validation results into clear, visual summaries.
• Set granular quality standards (warning, error, and critical levels) to trigger specific actions or alerts when data quality degrades.
• Seamlessly integrate with various data sources, including local data frames and remote databases like PostgreSQL, DuckDB, and Snowflake.

With fifty releases, we're thrilled to share that the pointblank ecosystem has matured into a stable, feature-rich solution for anyone prioritizing data governance and reproducible workflows.

Learn more about the journey to fifty releases and the future of pointblank: https://lnkd.in/gGbwqZ-m

Meet querychat: A Natural Language Interface for Your Data in Shiny

We are pleased to announce querychat, a new multilingual drop-in component designed to streamline data exploration for Python and R users within the Shiny ecosystem.

While traditional dashboards rely on predefined filters, querychat allows users to interact with data using natural language, translating human questions into precise SQL queries that execute directly within the application.

LLM Evals in Python: Moving Beyond "Vibes" to Systematic Testing

Because LLM outputs can change even with the same prompt, manual "vibe checks" are insufficient for production. LLM Evals aim to solve the problem of "non-determinism" and ensure model updates actually improve performance.

This approach helps catch regressions by:

• Isolating LLM logic so it can be tested and updated independently.
• Building realistic datasets of input and target pairs that cover expected behaviors and edge cases.
• Defining clear tasks and automated scoring rules to replace manual oversight.
• Integrating with CI to act as a quality gate for all model changes.

Karan Gathani explores how to create these Evals using Inspect AI for a Shiny for Python app in our recent technical post.

stringr 1.6.0: New String Transformation Utilities for R Users 🎻

The stringr package provides a cohesive set of functions designed to streamline string manipulation for R users. In case you missed it, the November release of stringr 1.6.0 added a few updates that you may be interested in!

• The update modifies how `str_replace_all()` handles function-based replacements. It now processes all values in a single character vector rather than calling the function for each individual string, so complex text transformations are a lot faster.
• `str_like()` is now case-sensitive by default. For case-insensitive matching, a new companion function, `str_ilike()`, has been introduced.
• My favorite update: stringr now includes dedicated functions to convert between common programming conventions, including `str_to_camel()`, `str_to_snake()`, and `str_to_kebab()`. 🐪 🐍 🍢

Announcing Orbital for Python 0.3.0: Accelerated Tree-Based Models in SQL

We are pleased to announce the release of Orbital for Python 0.3.0, a significant update to our library designed to streamline the deployment of machine learning models for Python and Scikit-learn users.

Orbital for Python allows you to transform Scikit-learn pipelines directly into native SQL queries, enabling model inference to execute within your database and eliminating the need for separate Python environments for production scoring. For those familiar with the R ecosystem, Orbital for R provides a similar capability that allows you to predict in databases using tidymodels workflows.

Version 0.3.0 optimizes tree-based models, addressing the challenge of long, complex SQL queries that can be difficult for database optimizers to parse and execute efficiently. Announcing gt 1.2.0: Enhanced Table Construction for Clinical Reporting

We are pleased to announce the release of gt v1.2.0, a package designed to streamline the creation of publication-quality tables for R users!

The updates in v1.2.0 are the result of a close collaboration between Posit and GSK, focusing on the complex reporting requirements often found in clinical and pharmaceutical industries (but that can benefit the larger user base, as well).

• You can now create hierarchical row labels by passing multiple columns to the stub, which is designed for representing parent-child relationships in clinical trials or financial reports, with automatic value consolidation for a cleaner visual hierarchy.
• The new summary_columns() function enables horizontal calculations (like row totals or averages) across multiple columns, complementing the existing vertical summary_rows() functionality.
• With fmt_number_si(), you can automatically format values using SI prefixes (k, M, G, etc.). 
• Significant improvements have been made to non-HTML outputs. Whether you are exporting to Word, LaTeX, or RTF, the new version ensures better text alignment, proper handling of Unicode characters, and consistent rendering of complex structures, such as column spanners.

The gt package continues to evolve as a comprehensive tool for data communication, and we look forward to you trying it out.

Announcing the bookdown.org Hosting Service Sunset on January 31, 2026

Since its launch in 2016, bookdown.org has served a vital role in hosting over 7,000 books made with the bookdown package. However, technology has advanced significantly since then. To best support the open source community with a scalable, modern environment, we have made the decision to decommission the bookdown.org website. This shift allows us to focus on supporting the community on Posit Connect Cloud, where we can provide enhanced features, reliability, and integration moving forward. Posit Connect Cloud provides:

• Unlimited, Free, Document Publishing: All users can publish an unlimited number of publicly shared documents for free.
• Polyglot Support: Publish R Markdown, Quarto, Shiny apps, and a host of Python frameworks on the same platform.
• Flexible Deployment: Deploy from your IDE or directly from GitHub.
• Paid Plans with Advanced Functionality: Gain access to advanced sharing and scheduling capabilities. 

This change only affects the free hosting service. The foundational bookdown R package will continue to be actively maintained and developed by Posit engineers.

Announcing testthat 3.3.0: Significant Enhancements for R Package Testing and Quality

We are pleased to announce the release of testthat 3.3.0, the latest update to the foundational testing framework for R. testthat is designed to help R package developers and data science teams turn informal checks into formal, automated tests that run quickly and reliably.

This release focuses on improving diagnostics, enhancing robustness, and ensuring compatibility with the modern R ecosystem (requiring R 4.1+), including:

• Enhanced Diagnostics: All expectations now provide clearer failure messages, consistently stating what was expected, what was received, and clearly illustrating the difference, greatly simplifying debugging.
• New Specific Expectations: Six new expectations have been introduced, including expect_all_true(), expect_disjoint(), and expect_shape(). These provide more precise error reporting than relying on general assertions like expect_true(all(...)).
• Test Randomization: The new shuffle argument for test_dir() and test_check() can randomly reorder top-level test expressions, helping to uncover and eliminate unintentional test dependencies.
• Performance Insight: Use the new SlowReporter (devtools::test(reporter = "slow")) to quickly identify and optimize the slowest tests in your package.

A critical update addresses lifecycle changes: the long-deprecated local_mock() and with_mock() functions have been removed, as they are incompatible with R 4.5.0+. Users should transition to the more robust local_mocked_bindings() and with_mocked_bindings() to future-proof their mocking strategies.

Learn more about testthat 3.3.0 and its full capabilities, including snapshot improvements and new vignettes: https://lnkd.in/gpuMvZFv

Announcing bluffbench: A New Evaluation Benchmark for LLM Plot Interpretation

bluffbench is a new evaluation tool designed to assess how well language models accurately describe data visualizations when plotted trends contradict their expectations.

In real-world data science, anomalies and unexpected patterns are often the most valuable discoveries. If LLM agents miss these subtle discrepancies that go against intuitive expectations, such as a lack of correlation where one is assumed, it can lead to incorrect conclusions or overlooked data quality issues.

bluffbench is specifically for those developing or using AI coding agents and data science tools that rely on LLMs to generate and interpret plots (such as Posit's Databot and Positron Assistant). It addresses the crucial challenge of LLMs exhibiting a tendency to "see what they expect to see" rather than accurately describing counterintuitive or anomalous data relationships presented in a plot.

Learn more about bluffbench and how LLMs see what they expect to see: https://lnkd.in/g-WGXf8T

Announcing plumber2 (0.1.0): The Next Generation R Web Server Framework

We are pleased to announce the release of plumber2 on CRAN. This package is the complete successor to the original plumber, designed to streamline the creation of web servers and REST APIs in R using both annotation-based and programmatic workflows.

plumber2 is specifically designed for R developers and Data Scientists seeking a consistent, high-performance way to share their R-based logic, models, or reports with internal systems and the wider world. It modernizes the API building process by adopting conventions R users are already familiar with (e.g., using roxygen2 directly for annotation parsing).

Other key features include:

• Easily convert standard handlers into non-blocking asynchronous ones using the @async tag, leveraging the mirai framework for better server performance and concurrency.
• Introduces native support for bidirectional communication via WebSockets, expanding R's capability beyond traditional REST APIs.
• Launch and serve Shiny applications or dynamically render Quarto/Rmarkdown documents directly from your plumber2 server.

If you are currently using plumber, we encourage you to explore plumber2 and begin migrating your services to this new and improved framework.

Learn more: https://lnkd.in/gbiTqYCz

We are pleased to (belatedly) announce the release of mall 0.2.0, a significant update to the tool designed to streamline Natural Language Processing (NLP) operations against your data using Large Language Models (LLMs).

Announcing ggplot2 4.0.0: A Significant Update to the R Visualization Framework

We are pleased to announce the release of ggplot2 4.0.0. This major update, which arrived on the package's 18th anniversary, includes substantial architectural shifts and a suite of features designed to improve the experience for R/Tidyverse data scientists and package developers alike.

This version focuses on making plot customization and labeling more intuitive, addressing the need for both simplified theming and a more robust foundation for extensibility.

Key features and benefits of ggplot2 4.0.0:

• Revamped theming system: Layer styling defaults are now controlled by themes using new "ink" and "paper" roles, leading to greater consistency. New helper functions like theme_sub_axis_x() are introduced to streamline verbose and often chaotic theme declarations.
• Smarter variable labeling: The package now uses the 'label' attribute from data column metadata (common in packages like gt and Hmisc) as the default label. You can also supply a labs(dictionary) for consistent, human-readable labels across plots.
• Architectural upgrade to S7: The core object system has been rewritten from S3 to S7. This provides a more robust structure, enforces stricter type checking, and enables powerful new capabilities for extension developers.

A New Resource for RStudio Users: Transitioning to Positron

Are you an RStudio user curious about the new Positron editor? We've published a helpful resource to guide you through the transition and showcase how Positron can enhance your data science workflow.

While RStudio is a stable and mature IDE, Positron is designed to be a polyglot editor that offers a smooth transition while providing native support for both R and Python.

The migration resources address key differences and provide guidance on how to:

• Handle projects: Positron uses a folder-based workspace model instead of .Rproj files, making it easier to work with multi-language projects.

Announcing Part 2 of "The Shiny Side of LLMs": A Guide to Talking with LLMs

We are pleased to announce the second part of the blog series, "Talking to LLMs: From Prompt to Response," designed for Python and R users who want to build real, useful LLM-powered applications, by Veerle Eeftink - van Leemput.

This post follows "Part 1: What LLMs Actually Do" and shifts the focus from theory to practical application. It details how to programmatically interact with LLMs using the chatlas (Python) and ellmer (R) packages, enabling users to move from simple chat conversations to building robust, automated systems.

The article addresses common challenges in building LLM applications by focusing on:

• Prompt Engineering: How to design effective prompts by defining the role, task, context, and output format for predictable, reliable results.
• Structured Output: Using data schemas (like Pydantic models in Python or type_* functions in R) to ensure the model's response is a perfectly parsable JSON object, ready for downstream processing.
• Tool Calling: Granting LLMs extra capabilities by enabling them to call external functions (tools) to perform tasks they're not good at, such as calculating precise metrics from a document.
• Model Parameters: Understanding how to control an LLM's behavior by adjusting settings like temperature and top-p for more creative or more deterministic outputs.

Learn how to talk to LLMs and build your own intelligent applications: https://lnkd.in/e6h3FF_4

Announcing Databot: An AI Assistant for Exploratory Data Analysis

We’re pleased to introduce Databot, our most ambitious application of LLMs to data science yet.

Databot is an AI assistant for exploratory data analysis (EDA) in Python and R, available now as a research preview in Positron. Announcing the Posit Publisher VS Code Extension 🚀

Posit Publisher deploys your R and Python content directly from your Visual Studio Code or Positron environment to Posit Connect.

It allows you to move from developing code to publishing a finished product, whether it's a Shiny app, a Quarto document, a Jupyter Notebook, or an API, for a more efficient, "code-first" deployment workflow.

• Publish your content to Posit Connect directly from your editor with a simple, integrated user interface.
• Manage deployment settings (titles, runtime processes, and dependencies) through configuration files saved in a .posit/ directory. This approach enhances reproducibility and collaboration across your team.
• Deploy a wide range of content, including Python APIs, applications, and notebooks, as well as R Shiny apps, R Markdown, and Quarto documents.
• With a shared configuration, your team can easily pick up where others left off, ensuring a consistent and reproducible deployment process for all collaborators.
• The extension supports managing multiple deployments for a single project, making it easy to deploy to staging and production environments with different settings.

Learn more about the Posit Publisher and install the extension:

We are excited to share that tidymodels now has sparsity support!

This support is for R and tidymodels users working with large datasets that contain many zero values, which are common across various analytical domains and often result from data preprocessing.

This update addresses the challenges of memory consumption and processing speed when handling sparse data by enabling recipe(), prep(), bake(), fit(), and predict() functions to work efficiently with sparse matrices by storing only the non-zero entries.

Announcing a streamlined MLOps workflow with Orbital on Databricks 🛰️ 

The Orbital package translates your Scikit-learn or tidymodels models into various backend formats, including native SQL. Your trained models can execute directly within a database, eliminating the need for separate environments for real-time inference and streamlining your MLOps workflow.

In Edgar Ruiz's blog post, he shows how to use Databricks as an integrated environment, along with Scikit-learn (for Python) and tidymodels (for R), all unified by the Orbital package.

• Develop your models on a sample of your data in Python or R, then use Orbital to translate the model into SQL.
• The resulting SQL statements are saved directly into your Databricks Workspace for immediate execution and can be efficiently scheduled as a Databricks Job. This allows you to transition models from experimentation to scheduled production runs without altering your original code.
• The workflow is similar for both Python and R users, allowing for consistent and efficient MLOps practices regardless of your preferred language.

Learn more in the post: https://lnkd.in/g5T5wcKd

We are pleased to announce Orbital for Python! Train your models within Scikit-learn, convert them to plain SQL, and then deploy them directly into your database.

Your model predictions can execute right within your database, eliminating the need for a separate Python environment for real-time inference. Orbital currently supports a variety of commonly used Scikit-learn models for regression and classification, including Linear Regression and Decision Trees.

Executing models via SQL within the database can offer significant performance benefits. For instance, running a model with Orbital on Snowflake has demonstrated up to a 5x speedup compared to running the same model in Python via Snowpark.

(And note, for those working in R, a similar capability was previously released for tidymodels!)

Announcing the release of Air 0.7.0, a significant update to the incredibly fast R formatter! 🎈 

Improvements and new features include:

• Enhanced Positron Integration: Air is now included by default in Positron, ensuring a seamless and up-to-date formatting experience right out of the box. Plus, usethis::use_air() makes project configuration a breeze! (pun intended)
• Introducing Autobracing: Say goodbye to inconsistent code! Autobracing automatically adds braces to if statements, loops, and function definitions, leading to more readable, consistent, and portable R code.
• Official GitHub Action: Automate your code formatting with the new setup-air GitHub Action. This makes it easier than ever to enforce consistent formatting across your projects and teams, even for external contributors.
• Flexible Formatting Control: New skip configuration in air.toml allows you to prevent formatting for specific functions, perfect for domain-specific languages or unique code structures.

Write clean, consistent, and maintainable code when developing in R. Check out the full announcement for all the details: https://lnkd.in/gExmQ58b

Large Language Models are powerful, but they generally lack access to your external info---unless you use tool calling. And with ellmer for R and chatlas for Python, if you can write a function, you can write an LLM tool!

Tool calling equips LLMs with "tools" to interact with your own data and systems.  Give LLMs access to APIs, databases, files, and more, letting them use up-to-date or user-specific information they wouldn't otherwise have. The LLM requests that the tool be run, ensuring your actual private data is never exposed to the model itself, only the schema.

This blog post by Sara Altman walks you through a practical calendar example, showing how you can easily define a tool, initialize a chat, and register your tool to let an LLM help with your schedule.

Check out "Easy tool calls with ellmer and chatlas": https://lnkd.in/gaPbwpjx

We're thrilled to share a fantastic project from Drew Coughlin that highlights how easy it is to build LLM-powered, queriable dashboards in Shiny using Posit's multilingual querychat package!

Drew combined his passion for trail running with some meticulous race data and Posit's powerful tools to create something truly useful for fellow ultra racing enthusiasts. His project evolved from a traditional Shiny dashboard into something far more flexible and intelligent, with querychat offering powerful features for a much broader audience interested in tabular data analysis.

Why querychat is a game-changer:

• LLMs understand your data without seeing it: You provide schema/descriptions, and it handles the rest – amazing for data privacy.
• SQL without the typing: Users can ask questions in plain language (e.g., "What percentage of runners over 40 completed the race each year?"), and querychat translates them into SQL queries, providing both the answer and the query.
• No LLM expertise required: You don't need to be an LLM expert. Just bring your tabular data, an API key, and a few simple configurations.

Drew's Shiny app, deployed on Posit Connect Cloud, shows just how straightforward it is to enhance analytics with LLMs.

Learn more about Drew's project and querychat here: https://lnkd.in/gHu4uC8D

Introducing Posit Connect Cloud for Organizations!
 
Posit Connect Cloud offers rapid Python and R code deployment to the cloud. Now, we're taking a leap forward for data teams everywhere with the new advanced plan for organizations.

This powerful new plan is designed to provide the essential tools and infrastructure your organization needs to collaborate effectively and securely. Here's what's included:

• Enterprise Single Sign-On (SSO): Seamlessly integrate with your existing identity providers like Okta and Microsoft Entra ID for secure, streamlined access.
• Role-Based Access Control (RBAC): Achieve granular control over content and account access, ensuring the right people have the right permissions.
• Secure External Sharing: Effortlessly grant access to external guests without requiring them to join your internal SSO system.

...and much more!

Whether you're a startup, a consulting group, or a large enterprise, Connect Cloud's Advanced plan is built to empower your data team to deploy and share a wide range of data frameworks with unparalleled flexibility and control.

We are delighted to announce mirai 2.3.0, for advancing async computing in R!

Designed for production, mirai is lightweight and lightning fast, featuring a true event-driven architecture and providing parallel and distributed computing over modern, scalable, and secure networking.

As the R ecosystem evolves, mirai leads the way in solving complex computing challenges:

• Offers an official base R parallel cluster type (as of R 4.5)
• Brings seamless event-driven async to Shiny for R
• Powers parallel processing in purrr (currently in its development version)

This latest release brings significant enhancements in:

• Serialization for multiple data types, such as Arrow Tables and torch tensors
• Capabilities for handling timeouts
• Comprehensive network discovery of IP addresses
• New utility functions for developers
• Extended support for users in HPC environments

Introducing Portable Linux R Binary Packages! 📦 

For years, Posit has been dedicated to reproducible data science, providing over 22,000 CRAN packages as Linux binaries through Posit Public Package Manager. But, we know system library dependencies can be a headache, especially in diverse Linux environments.

We're excited to preview a solution. Inspired by Python's manylinux and auditwheel projects, our new portable binary packages bundle system library dependencies directly with your R packages. They provide advantages such as:

• Improved user experience: Users no longer have to install runtime system library dependencies for R packages separately.
• Improved admin experience: Administrators no longer have to track down and install system library dependencies manually.
• Broad Linux support: Portable binary packages should be compatible with more Linux distributions than we currently support.
• Improved reproducibility: Bundling specific versions of these libraries ensures long-term reproducibility.
• Portability across R configurations: The new portable packages should be more broadly compatible with custom R builds.

Learn more about portable binary packages and how to install them in our latest blog post: https://lnkd.in/gdGA8cSr

With 193 active and future missions on the horizon and 18,000 civil servants to coordinate, NASA's workforce planning used to be challenging logistically.

Picture this: data scientists carrying their laptops from meeting room to meeting room, screen-sharing Python visualizations while mission planners tried to figure out staffing for the next decade.

Now? David Meza and NASA's People Analytics team have transformed that process with Posit Team.

Their "demand scenario forecaster" lets workforce planners simulate mission delays, skill shortages, and funding changes in real-time, allowing them to answer questions like "What impact will there be on our workforce if we delay this project by a year?"

The game changer here is speed. What used to take weeks of back and forth now happens in minutes.

From the Moon to Mars, every great leap for mankind starts with great planning. NASA - National Aeronautics and Space Administration is proving that the right data tools don't just support missions, they make them possible 🪐

With so many LLMs available to help you write R code, how do you choose the right one? We put a handful of models to the test using the vitals package, our framework for robust LLM evaluation.

We assessed their performance on a challenging dataset of R coding problems to find out which ones truly shine. Our conclusions: For a great balance of performance and cost, we recommend OpenAI's o4-mini or Anthropic's Claude Sonnet 4. While OpenAI's o3 performed best in our evaluation, it's significantly more expensive.

Many R programmers love Claude Sonnet, and it remains an excellent choice for R code generation!

Dive into the full analysis by Sara Altman and Simon P. Couch on the Posit Blog: https://lnkd.in/gyKvGj3z

Say hello to kuzco, a new LLM-powered computer vision assistant package in R!

Designed with local LLMs in mind, Frank Hull created kuzco to leverage Ollama to bring powerful computer vision capabilities right into your R workflow. It's perfect for anyone looking to streamline common computer vision tasks and get results in a tidy data frame format.

kuzco currently supports:

• Classification: kuzco combines computer vision with LLMs to provide richer, contextual understanding of your images.
• Recognition: Unlike broad classification, kuzco accurately detects and identifies individual objects within an image.
• Sentiment Analysis: kuzco interprets the underlying emotional tone and context of your visual data.
• Text Extraction: Pull text directly from images, turning visual information into readable data.
• Alt-Text Generation: Create descriptive and accessible alt-text for your images.

Frank demonstrates how to integrate cutting-edge computer vision into your R projects in the latest post on the Posit Blog: https://lnkd.in/gAC8NxxT

Last year, we brought the Posit Workbench Native App to Snowflake! 

Now, R and Python users can open up RStudio, Jupyter, or VS Code and work directly inside Snowflake, right next to their data, with Snowflake’s security and access controls built in.

Since that launch, we’ve added even more ways to use Posit with Snowflake.

1️⃣ Connected Apps
Use Posit Workbench or Posit Connect from anywhere and securely query Snowflake.

2️⃣ Open Source Tools
Use R and Python packages that take advantage of Snowflake’s compute, like:
- orbital, for turning R or Python models into SQL that runs in Snowflake
- gander, chores, chatlas, and querychat, which connect with Snowflake Cortex for secure, context-aware AI assistance

3️⃣ Native Apps
Run Posit Workbench entirely inside your Snowflake account using Snowpark Container Services.

And inside the Posit Workbench Native App, you’ll find tools pre-installed to help you get started faster:

⚡️gander: is a chat and code completion experience, like Copilot, that knows how to query your R environment to write data science code that is ready to use.
⚡️chores: provides a library of ergonomic LLM assistants that help you quickly complete repetitive, hard-to-automate tasks.
⚡️ellmer: makes it easy to use large language models (LLM) from R. It supports a wide variety of LLM providers and implements a rich set of features including streaming outputs, tool/function calling, structured data extraction, and more.

Why are teams adopting it? Teams are getting insights faster, reducing IT friction, strengthening their security posture, and empowering data scientists to focus on delivering value, not wrestling with infrastructure and access.

We’re continuing to build with Snowflake. Here are some things we're working on right now.

⚡️Private Link support
⚡️Posit Connect as a Native App (coming soon)
⚡️The next-gen Positron IDE inside the Posit Workbench Native App (coming soon)

If you’re attending Snowflake Summit, stop by booth 1903. We’ll show you how to build and deploy a GenAI-powered Shiny app within Snowflake.

Read more about what’s new and what’s next with Snowflake on our most recent blog → https://lnkd.in/dZMNJXn2

And inside the Posit Workbench Native App, you’ll find tools pre-installed to help you get started faster:

⚡️gander: is a chat and code completion experience, like Copilot, that knows how to query your R environment to write data science code that is ready to use.
⚡️chores: provides a library of ergonomic LLM assistants that help you quickly complete repetitive, hard-to-automate tasks.
⚡️ellmer: makes it easy to use large language models (LLM) from R. It supports a wide variety of LLM providers and implements a rich set of features including streaming outputs, tool/function calling, structured data extraction, and more.

Why are teams adopting it? Teams are getting insights faster, reducing IT friction, strengthening their security posture, and empowering data scientists to focus on delivering value, not wrestling with infrastructure and access.

We’re continuing to build with Snowflake. Here are some things we're working on right now.

⚡️Private Link support
⚡️Posit Connect as a Native App (coming soon)
⚡️The next-gen Positron IDE inside the Posit Workbench Native App (coming soon)

If you’re attending Snowflake Summit, stop by booth 1903. We’ll show you how to build and deploy a GenAI-powered Shiny app within Snowflake.

Read more about what’s new and what’s next with Snowflake on our most recent blog → https://lnkd.in/dZMNJXn2

We’re excited to announce the release of Posit Workbench and RStudio 2025.05.0!

Updates for developers include:

• Quarto 1.6 integration for powerful new features like centralized branding (brand.yml), improved RevealJS navigation, flexible content layout options (contents shortcode, landscape blocks), and enhanced cross-referencing.
• Enhanced GitHub Copilot Integration that provides more relevant suggestions using project context, supports completions within Quarto visual mode code chunks, and warns users when they approach free tier limits.
• Positron Pro (Preview) with improved runtime detection, easier publishing to Connect via the stable Posit Publisher extension, an enhanced Data Explorer (with direct file opening, loading indicators, better tooltips), and experimental multi-session console capabilities.
• RStudio IDE Improvements for clearer console output distinguishing errors/warnings/messages and more robust .Rproj file handling to minimize version control churn (less frequent writing of ProjectId), drag-and-drop file opening (Desktop), digitally signed Windows binaries, and accessibility improvements.

Updates for administrators include:

• Admin Configuration UI (Preview), anew graphical interface to simplify common Workbench configuration tasks.
• Workbench now includes an official policy module, enabling support for SELinux-enforced environments.
• Audited Jobs (Preview) enables users to run R scripts with cryptographically signed outputs and environment details for verifiable, reproducible results, crucial for regulated use cases.
• Positron Pro (Preview) improves R and Python environment discovery and adds a stable Posit Publisher extension for sharing data apps and other output.

To learn more about what’s included in this release, read our blog post highlighting all of the key additions: https://lnkd.in/gu_ku7Ax

How did migrating a codebase to Polars reduce costs by 98%?

Jeroen Janssens and Thijs Nieuwdorp detailed their work at Xomnia with client Alliander (a major utilities company in the Netherlands) to process vast network data, where a single network could take over five hours and 500GB of RAM.

Moving to Polars resulted in a dramatic improvement, with processing time for a single network reducing to four hours (a 20% reduction despite doubling the samples), RAM usage plummeting by 92% to just 40GB, and the entire low voltage grid calculation costing significantly less than the budget.

This is in part thanks to:

• Benchmarking: To convince stakeholders, they demonstrated the performance benefits of Polars by benchmarking small pieces of code, showing a reduction from 30 seconds to about one second for a translated pandas snippet.
• Polars' lazy API for performance gains: Computations are deferred and optimized compared to the eager API.
• Strategic caching: Caching or persisting intermediate results prevents redundant computations of heavy operations within the query plan
• Polars' simplified API: Thanks to simplified string handling, consistent representation of missing values, and a lack of index, working with Polars meant a more maintainable codebase.

Come for the speed, stay for the API!

Watch the recording here: https://lnkd.in/esNTVaMN

We are thrilled to announce Air, an extremely fast R formatter!

A code formatter automates code layout by enforcing predefined style guidelines for whitespace, line breaks, and punctuation. While preserving the code's functionality, it ensures consistent formatting, leading to improved readability, adherence to length constraints, better visual organization of syntax, and reduced merge conflicts in team-based projects.

Written in Rust, Air seamlessly integrates with your editor, offering "Format on Save" and "Format Selection" features. Its command-line interface supports formatting individual files, recursive directory formatting, and a --check mode ideal for Git pre-commit hooks or GitHub Actions workflows.

Learn more about Air on the tidyverse blog: https://lnkd.in/gNBZdDQB

Introducing chores, a new package that helps you with tedious but hard-to-automate tasks using large language models (LLMs)!
 
Do you have a 1-minute data science task that you do all of the time? Could you teach another data scientist to do it with a couple of paragraphs of explanation and an example or two? With chores, you organize these tasks as "chores": markdown files containing clear instructions or "prompts." Select a chore from the dropdown, and it sends the corresponding prompt along with your selected code to the LLM to execute the task.
 
Simon P. Couch introduces the package and demonstrates how to scaffold minimal roxygen2 documentation in this blog post: https://lnkd.in/gpAn8bCm

We are excited for ShinyConf 2025! There are dozens of talks to look forward to, but let's highlight one talk by Charlie Gao: "Scaling Shiny: Seamless and Effortless Distributed Computing with mirai".

Charlie, a software engineer on Posit's open source team, will showcase how mirai natively supports Shiny ExtendedTask, leveraging async innovations to drive high performance and minimize latency for scaling Shiny applications.

Abstract: Distributed computing using Shiny ExtendedTask offers a complementary technique to traditional horizontal scaling methods. Distributing only the most compute-intensive or time-consuming elements offers a more efficient solution than simply replicating entire applications. It is also the only way to ensure the best user experience by leveraging intra-session async to keep user sessions responsive.

Natively supporting Shiny ExtendedTask, mirai provides the latest async innovations that drive the highest performance and push latency to the absolute minimum. mirai is the only solution to include both modern networking and true event-driven async. This all helps to ensure that Shiny ExtendedTask lives up to its promise of delivering a seamless user experience, even for heavy and complicated workloads.

An even more exciting prospect lies in mirai’s ability to open up the power of distributed computing – with an unprecedented ease of use. This means that it is effectively just as easy to distribute tasks across enterprise cloud deployments, as it is to utilize spare compute on a local network. mirai can create TLS-secured connections, generating certificates on-the-fly with no intervention required. mirai’s integrated launchers can also use existing SSH setups to create connections over tunnelled SSH, without the need to modify firewall settings or any other system configuration.

We hope to see you there!

We are excited for ShinyConf 2025! There are dozens of talks to look forward to, but let's highlight one talk by Daniel Chen: "Creating and Sharing Scalable Applications with Shiny"

Daniel - a developer evangelist at Posit – will discuss the challenges of building large-scale Shiny apps and how to use Shiny modules to create reusable components and refactor complex code, emphasizing best practices for both Shiny for R and Python.

Abstract: Shiny provides a framework for building interactive web applications, enabling seamless integration with data science libraries. It offers a reactive programming model, allowing users to create dynamic dashboards and applications with minimal code.

However, when applications get larger, you may end up repeating UI elements or have complex reactive logic. To make the codebase sustainable, we can turn to shiny modules.

Shiny modules allow us to create functions that we can package up that still retain the reactive context. This allows us to refactor our shiny code as if it were any other piece of data analysis code. This talk will give a practical example of refactoring code into a shiny module that you can then turn into a separate package. We will use Shiny for Python as the working example in the talk, but the concepts will apply to Shiny for R

We hope to see you there!

We are excited for ShinyConf 2025! There are dozens of talks to look forward to, but let's highlight one talk by Garrick Aden-Buie: "Theming Made Easy: Introducing brand.yml for Shiny"

Garrick – a software engineer on Posit's Shiny team – will introduce brand.yml, a simple YAML file that streamlines styling for Shiny (in R or Python) and beyond, ensuring a consistent and professional look across applications, reports, and presentations.

Abstract: In the world of Shiny app development, effective communication is paramount. For your insights to truly resonate and drive decision-making, your Shiny apps need to be more than just functional – they need to project a professional image that aligns with your organization's brand. Achieving this consistent and professional look can be surprisingly challenging. That’s why we’re excited to introduce brand.yml, a simple YAML file that streamlines styling for Shiny — in R or Python – and beyond.

In this talk, we’ll discover just how easy it is to set up brand.yml for your Shiny for R apps. brand.yml consolidates key visual elements – colors, fonts, and logos – into a single, easily managed file, helping ensure that even your smallest Shiny apps feel authentically on-brand. We’ll also demonstrate how that same file can be used not only in Shiny for R and Python but also to theme your reports, slides, dashboards, and apps in Quarto or R Markdown and more.

You spend countless hours making sure that your Shiny apps bring insights to life through engaging interactivity and effective communication. With brand.yml, you can create Shiny apps that inspire confidence with a consistent and polished visual identity.

We hope to see you there!

Generating realistic datasets is useful in various settings, from education to app testing. Sara Altman's new blog post explores how you can leverage Large Language Models (LLMs) directly within R to generate custom, realistic datasets using the ellmer package.

This blog post provides a step-by-step guide to creating a command-line tool in R that simulates data and embedding the data generation functionality in a Shiny app, highlighting key tasks like:

• Starting a chat with the `chat_*()` functions, essentially a record of the conversation between the user and the chatbot.
• Specifying a system prompt to instruct the LLM to generate tabular data based on the criteria given by the user.
• Using tool calling to ensure that the LLM generates correctly formatted data and can automatically retry if an error occurs.
• Adding these functionality to a Shiny app.

We are thrilled to announce the release of chatlas, a Python package designed to simplify interacting with large language models (LLM) in Python!

LLMs offer an opportunity to transform the way we work. However, LLM APIs tend to be designed for AI experts, putting functionality over simplicity. 

chatlas aims to make LLMs more accessible (especially to data scientists) by making common tasks easy. As an added bonus, chatlas has an identical interface to the ellmer R package, making it easy to translate your LLM projects between R and Python.

Chatlas allows Python data scientists to:

• Chat through an interactive chat console, interactive chat method, or a stream method (for example, to create a Shiny chatbot).
• Extend LLM capabilities by integrating external Python functions with tool calling.
• Pass a pydantic model to extract structured data from free-form text and images.
• Benefit from autocompletion in your editor with chatlas’s comprehensive typing support.
• Use Retrieval-Augmented Generation (RAG) to improve LLM responses.
Run logging mechanisms to monitor and debug your chatbot in production.

We’re actively developing chatlas and have exciting plans for the future. We encourage you to try it out, and you can find useful background information and motivating examples in the “Getting Started” documentation.

Learn more in the blog post: https://lnkd.in/gHMDt9qX

We are excited for ShinyConf 2025! There are dozens of talks to look forward to, but let's highlight one talk by Carson Sievert: "Building LLM-Powered Shiny apps via ellmer and chatlas"

Carson - an engineer on Posit's Shiny team – will highlight Posit's R package ellmer and its Python counterpart chatlas, which simplify common LLM tasks, and inspire ideas for Shiny AI apps.

Abstract: Large Language Models (LLMs) offer an opportunity to transform the way we work for the better. To help understand why and how, it helps immensely to build software that utilizes them. Unfortunately, this can be challenging – LLM APIs tend to be designed for AI experts, putting functionality over simplicity. At Posit, we're working to make LLMs more accessible through the R package ellmer and its sister Python package chatlas. They aim to make common tasks like streaming output, tool calling, data extraction, etc. extremely easy. Combined with Shiny, it's easier than ever to build sophisticated chatbots and other gen-AI experiences. In this talk, I highlight the best parts of ellmer and chatlas, provide inspiration for your next Shiny AI app, and share why AI will make code-first data scientists more valuable than ever.

We hope to see you there!

We're delighted to announce that Jonathan McPherson – software architect at Posit PBC – will be a keynote speaker at posit::conf(2025)!

Jonathan will talk about Positron: 10 years of data science IDEs, what happens next? 
Drawing on his decade of experience developing the RStudio IDE and leading that team, he will reflect on the foundational principles that have made RStudio the standard data science environment for R. He will discuss how these guiding principles inform the development of Positron, Posit's new data science environment (in beta testing, positron.posit.co). Attendees can look forward to insights into Positron's advancements and a glimpse into the potential future of data science tools in the coming decade.

If you are a data scientist, and if you're curious about how thoughtful design principles can improve the tools you use, you won't want to miss this!

The promise of AI in data science workflows—now made seamless and secure.

AI has transformed software development, making developers faster and more efficient. Large Language Models (LLMs) can suggest, complete, and improve code as it’s written.

But if you’ve tried using ChatGPT or another LLM for R-based data science, you’ve likely hit some frustrating roadblocks:

❌ Context switching fatigue—jumping between your code and an external AI tool.
❌ Security concerns—moving sensitive data outside your trusted environment.
❌ Lack of project awareness—LLMs generate pseudo code and dummy variables instead of relevant suggestions.

So, is a seamless, secure, and truly intelligent AI assistant for R-based data science possible?

Yes, it is! The Posit Native App on Snowflake SPCS works with R packages like gander and chores to provide relevant code suggestions powered by Snowflake Cortex.

Using gander and chores with the Posit Native App in SPCS is a natural choice because:

✔️ You can start using AI immediately with no friction.
✔️ You stay secure and compliant because data never leaves your Snowflake ecosystem.
✔️ You reduce the friction of context switching. Both gander and chores can seamlessly interact with Cortex models through the press of a keyboard shortcut in RStudio.
✔️ You receive better, more targeted suggestions from your AI, which reduces the time spent on iteration and refinement.

🔗 Check out a demo of how it all works together → https://lnkd.in/gh9rnEaJ

We are excited for ShinyConf 2025! There are dozens of talks to look forward to, but let's highlight one talk by Barret Schloerke: "AI Kung-Fu: Training your model in Two Function Calls"

Barret – an engineer on the Shiny Team at Posit – will demonstrate how to create system prompts for Python packages using just two function calls, enabling LLMs to interact with custom tools seamlessly.

Abstract: Teaching an AI how to use your newly developed Python package can be challenging. Large Language Models (LLMs) excel at generating code but lack the knowledge to interact seamlessly with every existing package. In this talk, I'll demonstrate how to create a comprehensive system prompt for your Python package using just two function calls. This generated prompt guides the LLM on how to interact with your specific package and only requires a few manual instructions. To illustrate the efficacy of this approach, we'll integrate the generated prompt into the 'chatlas' Python package and engage in a live chat with the LLM.

We hope to see you there!

We are thrilled to announce Pointblank for data validation in Python!

Data validation assesses the accuracy and quality of data before it gets used. It ensures that only “good” data gets through by setting rules and checks to catch errors, inconsistencies, and missing values. And, as Richard Iannone says, "good data quality strongly determines the quality of decisions."

Pointblank checks your data against certain expectations and constraints, then presents the results in a beautiful validation report table. You can also obtain fine-grained results of the interrogation as individual step reports or via methods that provide key metrics.

To make this all work seamlessly, Pointblank uses Narwhals to work with Polars and Pandas DataFrames. It also integrates with Ibis to enable the use of DuckDB, MySQL, PostgreSQL, SQLite, Parquet, and more.

Learn more in the blog post: https://lnkd.in/g-hhun6c

Introducing gander, the AI-powered coding assistant that sees into your R environment!

Tired of large language models (LLMs) guessing about your R data? gander automatically describes objects, including column names, types, and distributions, so that LLMs have the context they need for effective coding assistance. It's integrated with RStudio and Positron for a high-performance and low-friction chat experience.

Simon P. Couch walks through the motivation behind gander and demonstrates a practical example using stackoverflow data on the Posit Blog: https://lnkd.in/gF5UN_8W

Winston will explore the intersection of LLMs and Shiny for building powerful web applications and how AI can enhance the Shiny development process itself.

I will talk about some of the tools we've developed at Posit to help you build applications that use LLMs, and I'll show you how simple it is to get started building your first AI chat application with Shiny.

We're excited to announce that a new release of odbc is on CRAN!

The goal of the odbc package is to provide a DBI-compliant interface to ODBC drivers. This makes it easy to connect databases such as SQL Server, Oracle, Databricks, and Snowflake.

This latest release includes:

• An awesome new hex logo thanks to Edgar Ruiz
• Viewer-based credentials on Posit Connect for `databricks()` and `snowflake()`
• A new `redshift()` helper to simplify connections to Amazon Redshift clusters
• Many QOL improvements for Databricks, Snowflake, MSSQL, etc.

Read more in the release notes: https://lnkd.in/gpz5qMur

Simon P. Couch has been exploring the fascinating intersection of Large Language Models (LLMs) and R package development, and the results are truly inspiring! He's shared his journey and the experimental packages he's created on the tidyverse blog: https://lnkd.in/gTJvPWer

He discusses three new R packages, chores, ensure, and gander, designed to boost productivity in R package development and data science by automating repetitive tasks using Large Language Models (LLMs).

• chores (previously pal): Automates tasks via keyboard shortcuts, like updating testing code and drafting roxygen documentation.
• ensure: Focuses on writing unit tests by understanding function arguments from the entire file and neighboring files.
• gander: A chat interface that allows models to "see" the data being worked on, tailoring responses for data scientists.

These packages are still experimental, but they showcase the immense potential of LLMs to revolutionize our workflows. Try out ellmer, chores, ensure, and gander yourself, and share your experiences!

Creating tables in Python keeps getting better! 

With the Great Tables package, you can create polished and professional display tables in Python. Since its debut, the package has seen a flurry of updates and new features. We round up some of the news that you may have missed in our latest blog post, including how to:

• Download Great Tables as an image file
• Embed Great Tables in an email/HTML webpage
• Output LaTeX in Great Tables
• Add strings wherever there are nulls in Great Tables
• Create interactive tables in Python with reactable-py 

Read the blog post here: https://lnkd.in/gjnsre4z

As a relatively new package, we’re eager to hear your feedback! Questions, feature requests, or bug reports. Let us know on our community Discord server: https://lnkd.in/g_WqrxDK

We are delighted to announce that Reticulate 1.41 is now on CRAN!

The reticulate package provides a comprehensive set of tools for interoperability between Python and R. In v1.41, we’re introducing a new approach that eliminates the need for manual installation and
environment management. 

Gone are the days of manually setting up virtual environments. You can now simply declare your dependencies using `py_require()`, and Reticulate will handle the rest. 

This seamless experience is powered by uv, an extremely fast Python package manager written in Rust. 

Declare your requirements, and Reticulate—powered by uv—does the rest 💜 

Read more about it on the blog: https://lnkd.in/gst484vs
